question: Tell us about a time you worked as part of a team. What was your role and how did it go?
lookingFor: Evidence of genuine collaboration — awareness of others' contributions, not just your own output.

bullets: Name the team, the goal, and your specific role
Show how you contributed while supporting the group
End with what the team achieved and what you personally learned
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: In my final year we ran a school fundraiser and I volunteered to coordinate the volunteer rota — not the most glamorous job, but someone had to hold it together. I quickly realised the team worked in different rhythms, so I built a shared spreadsheet and checked in briefly every few days rather than calling constant meetings. We raised 40% more than the previous year, and I came away understanding that good coordination is mostly about removing friction for other people, not directing them.

commonMistakes: Describing a team project as if you did it alone
Being so vague about your role it sounds like you had none
Focusing only on the outcome and ignoring what you learned
followUps: What would the rest of the team say about your contribution?

question: Describe a time you took the lead on something. What did you do and what happened?
lookingFor: A concrete example of stepping up — taking ownership and guiding others toward a goal, not just being in charge by title.

bullets: Set up why leadership was needed and why you stepped in
Describe what you actually did to move the group forward
Share the outcome and an honest reflection on your approach
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Our geography fieldwork group lost its chosen site two weeks before the deadline and everyone froze. I suggested we reframe it and use the urban canal nearby instead — it actually fit our theme better. I divvied up the data collection so no one person was overloaded, and we turned in the project on time with stronger primary data than we'd planned. Looking back I could have asked for more input on the new direction rather than proposing it and moving fast, but the group told me afterwards that the decisiveness was what we needed in that moment.

commonMistakes: Conflating having a title (head boy/girl, captain) with actually leading
Skipping the reflection — what did you learn about your leadership style
Describing a situation where no real leadership challenge existed
followUps: How would you lead that group differently if you had to do it again?

question: Tell us about a time you had too much on your plate at once. How did you manage it?
lookingFor: A practical prioritisation approach — not 'I worked harder' but evidence of genuine triage and decision-making under pressure.

bullets: Name the competing demands clearly so the panel understands the load
Explain how you decided what to do first and why
Show the result and what the experience taught you about managing yourself
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: At the same time as my mock exams, I was finishing a long-term volunteering commitment at a literacy charity that had a key session I'd promised to lead. I listed everything, worked out what had real deadlines versus what just felt urgent, and protected three solid revision blocks a day while cutting out anything discretionary. For the volunteering session I prepared earlier than usual so I wasn't cramming the night before. Both came together — I actually used the sessions as a mental break from revision. The main thing I learned is that a written list takes the panic out of busyness.

commonMistakes: Just saying 'I stayed organised and worked harder' with no specifics
Describing a situation that isn't actually that demanding
Admitting you let something important drop without reflecting on what you'd do differently
followUps: What do you do now to avoid getting into that position in the first place?

question: Tell us about a time you received critical feedback. How did you respond?
lookingFor: Emotional maturity — the ability to hear difficult feedback without getting defensive and to act on it constructively.

bullets: Name the feedback specifically, not vaguely
Be honest about your initial reaction before describing what you did next
Show the concrete change you made and what came of it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My history teacher told me my essays were too descriptive and not analytical enough — basically that I was telling the story instead of making an argument. My first reaction was to feel a bit stung because I'd put real effort in, but I sat with it and realised she was right. I asked her to annotate one essay so I could see it at sentence level, not just as a general comment. I started every paragraph with a claim, not a fact, and my grades went up a full band by the end of term. The most useful thing wasn't the feedback itself — it was asking for the specific example.

commonMistakes: Pretending you had no emotional reaction — it sounds rehearsed
Picking trivial feedback that doesn't actually challenge you
Describing the change you made without showing whether it worked
followUps: Have you ever disagreed with feedback you were given? What did you do?

question: Tell us about a problem you had to solve on the spot, without much time to prepare. What did you do?
lookingFor: Quick, structured thinking — a demonstration that you can stay calm, gather what you need, and act decisively when plans fall apart.

bullets: Set the scene so the time pressure is clear
Walk through your thinking process step by step
Evaluate your solution honestly — what worked, what you'd change
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I was chairing a school debate when the main opposition speaker went home sick an hour before. I had twenty minutes before the audience arrived. I quickly found the most prepared reserve speaker, gave him a five-minute brief on the key arguments, and restructured the format slightly so the burden on each speaker was lighter. The debate ran, it was actually pretty lively, and afterwards several people said they hadn't realised anything had changed. What I noticed is that in a crisis the most useful skill is knowing what you can fix and letting go of what you can't.

commonMistakes: Describing a situation that wasn't really on-the-spot — you had time to prepare
A solution that just boils down to 'I panicked and it worked out'
No reflection on what the experience taught you
followUps: What would you have done if your fix hadn't worked either?

question: Tell us about something you taught yourself, outside of school, because you wanted to.
lookingFor: Intrinsic motivation and the ability to learn independently — a signal that you'll thrive in a university environment where no one tells you what to do.

bullets: Name what you learned and why you chose it
Describe how you went about it — what resources, what process
Connect it to something broader — curiosity, a project, your studies
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I taught myself basic Python over one summer because I wanted to analyse the football match data I'd been collecting as a hobby for two years. I started with free online tutorials, got stuck constantly, but found that debugging the errors was actually when I learned the most. By the end I'd built a small script that visualised our school team's patterns over a season. It wasn't elegant code, but it answered a question I actually cared about, which I think is why I stuck with it when the tutorials alone would have bored me.

commonMistakes: Naming a class or course you were guided through — that's directed learning
Picking something trivial that doesn't reveal much curiosity
Failing to connect it to your broader interests or goals
followUps: What made you persist when it got difficult?

question: Tell us about a time something didn't go to plan. How did you adapt?
lookingFor: Flexibility and a positive orientation to uncertainty — adapting with purpose, not just surviving.

bullets: Name the original plan and what actually changed
Describe the adjustment you made and why
Reflect on whether the change ultimately helped or hurt, and what that taught you
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I spent months preparing a biology extended project on local pond ecosystems, then in March the site was fenced off for construction without warning. I had two options: wait and probably miss the deadline, or pivot. I switched to a comparison using published data from three different UK pond studies instead, which meant rethinking my methodology from scratch. The examiner later said the comparative approach was actually stronger than a single-site study would have been. I don't think I'd have chosen that angle if I hadn't been forced to, so the disruption ended up serving the work.

commonMistakes: Picking a trivial change — the bar should show genuine disruption
Framing adaptation as passive ('I just had to go with it') rather than active
Not addressing whether the outcome was actually good
followUps: Do you think you're naturally good at adapting, or is it something you have to work at?

question: Tell us about a time you persuaded someone to change their mind. How did you do it?
lookingFor: Evidence of reasoned persuasion — using evidence and listening, not pressure or persistence alone.

bullets: Describe the original disagreement and the stakes
Explain your approach — listening first, then evidence or reframing
Show the outcome and what made the persuasion work
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Our student magazine editor wanted to drop a piece I'd written on school mental health because he thought it was too sensitive. I asked him what specifically worried him, and it turned out he was concerned about one anecdote that was identifiable. I offered to anonymise that section and add a short note about where students could get support. He agreed and the piece ran. The lesson for me was that he wasn't opposed to the topic — he had a specific concern I'd not addressed. Once I understood what he actually needed, the persuasion was straightforward.

commonMistakes: Framing it as 'I argued until they gave in' — that's not persuasion
Taking all the credit and ignoring the other person's legitimate concern
Picking a case where the stakes were so low it shows nothing
followUps: Are there situations where you'd choose not to try to persuade someone?

question: Tell us about a time you had to work closely with someone whose style was very different from yours. How did you make it work?
lookingFor: Emotional intelligence and adaptability — the ability to bridge a style gap rather than just tolerating friction.

bullets: Name the style difference concretely without dismissing the other person
Describe the adjustment you made to your own approach
Show the result and what it changed about how you think about collaboration
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My lab partner for our chemistry coursework was incredibly detail-oriented — she wanted to document every variable before we started, and I tend to learn by doing first and refining later. Early on this caused real frustration on both sides. I suggested we split the work by instinct: she led the planning and write-up, I ran the initial trials and flagged what needed adjusting. Her rigour caught errors I'd have glossed over, and my pace stopped us over-planning. The final write-up was honestly better than either of us would have produced alone. That experience changed how I think about complementary styles.

commonMistakes: Framing the other person as the problem without any self-reflection
Saying 'I just kept my head down' — that's tolerance, not collaboration
No concrete change in your own behaviour
followUps: What would you do if adapting your style wasn't enough to close the gap?

question: Describe a time you were criticised in front of others. How did you handle it?
lookingFor: Composure and professional response — taking the feedback seriously without shutting down or reacting defensively in a public setting.

bullets: Set the scene — who was there and what was said
Describe your immediate response and how you managed your reaction
Show what you did with the feedback afterwards
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I gave a practice presentation and my teacher stopped me midway to point out I was reading from my notes rather than engaging with the audience — in front of about twenty classmates. My instinct was to flush and rush to finish, but I paused and said, 'You're right, let me start that section again.' It immediately felt better, and the class was noticeably more engaged. Afterwards I reworked the whole deck to prompt rather than script, and the final presentation was one of my strongest. The embarrassing moment in the middle was probably the reason.

commonMistakes: Saying you were unbothered — a total lack of reaction is not believable
Focussing on whether the criticism was fair rather than how you responded
No follow-up action — just surviving the moment isn't enough
followUps: Do you think public criticism is more or less useful than private feedback?

question: Tell us about a goal you set for yourself. How did you go about achieving it?
lookingFor: Evidence of self-directed ambition and follow-through — not just the outcome, but the process of working toward something deliberately.

bullets: Name the goal and why it mattered to you personally
Walk through how you broke it down and stayed on track
Be honest about setbacks and how you kept going
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I set myself the goal of qualifying for regional athletics at 1500m, knowing I was well outside the standard at the time. I worked with my coach to break the target into monthly splits, which made it feel achievable rather than arbitrary. There were weeks when I was tired from studying and skipped sessions — I learned to track those gaps and make up the quality if not the volume. I qualified with three seconds to spare in April. What I value most about that year isn't the time — it's that I proved to myself I can build toward something slowly when I believe in it.

commonMistakes: Picking a goal that was set by someone else for you
Describing only the outcome without explaining the process
Implying it was straightforward — setbacks are part of what makes it credible
followUps: Have you ever set a goal and given up? What made the difference?

question: Tell us about a time you had to explain something complex to someone who didn't have your background. How did you approach it?
lookingFor: The ability to translate knowledge across an expertise gap — a skill that matters in seminars, presentations, and eventually professional life.

bullets: Name what you were explaining and who you were explaining it to
Describe the approach — analogies, checking understanding, adjusting
Show whether it worked and what the experience taught you about communication
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I volunteered to explain compound interest to a group of Year 8 students as part of a school finance day. I started with the textbook definition and immediately saw blank faces. I scrapped it and asked them about pocket money instead — if they spent it all each week versus saved and it grew by a small amount each week, what would the difference be in a year? We worked through it on a whiteboard together with real numbers they'd chosen. By the end they were asking their own questions. I learned that clarity isn't about simplifying what you know — it's about connecting it to what they already understand.

commonMistakes: Picking an explanation that was straightforward, not truly complex
Describing a one-way monologue rather than a two-way process
Showing no awareness of whether the other person actually understood
followUps: What would you do if someone still didn't understand after your explanation?

question: Tell us about a difficult decision you had to make. How did you reach it?
lookingFor: A structured decision-making process — weighing options, accepting trade-offs, and owning the outcome.

bullets: Describe why it was genuinely difficult — real competing options or values
Walk through your process: what information you sought, who you consulted
Own the outcome and reflect on whether you'd decide the same way again
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: In my second year I had to choose between staying in the school orchestra — something I'd done for four years — and dropping it to protect time for my A-level preparations. Both mattered to me and dropping music felt like losing part of myself. I talked to my teacher, looked honestly at my revision hours, and decided I needed to leave the orchestra but keep playing privately. I don't regret it, but it wasn't painless. I think the most useful part of the process was distinguishing between 'I'd feel guilty giving this up' and 'this genuinely serves my long-term goals' — those are different things.

commonMistakes: Picking a decision that was objectively obvious — not genuinely hard
Describing a decision you made alone with no consideration of other perspectives
Pretending the decision had no cost or downside
followUps: Looking back, do you think you used a good process even if the outcome is uncertain?

question: Tell us about a time you supported a classmate or peer who was struggling. What did you do?
lookingFor: Empathy in action — genuine attentiveness to someone else's need and a response that put them first.

bullets: Name the situation and how you noticed the other person was struggling
Describe what you did specifically — not just 'I was there for them'
Reflect on what you learned about helping others well
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A close friend on my course was falling behind after a family illness and was too embarrassed to ask for notes. I noticed she'd been quieter than usual, asked her directly one lunchtime, and she opened up. I shared my notes without making a big deal of it, and we started doing a short catch-up every Sunday for a few weeks so she could ask questions without the pressure of a classroom. She caught up before the mocks. What I learned is that most people who are struggling won't ask — if you wait for them to speak, you often wait too long.

commonMistakes: Describing help you gave that was really about your own convenience
Being vague — 'I supported them' with no description of what that looked like
Making yourself the hero of someone else's story
followUps: Is there a point where supporting someone starts to hold you back? How do you find that line?

question: Tell us about a time you went beyond what was expected of you. What motivated you?
lookingFor: Intrinsic drive rather than external reward — doing more because the work or the cause mattered, not to be noticed.

bullets: Name clearly what the minimum expectation was and what you actually did
Explain why — without making it sound like a performance for praise
Reflect on whether going beyond was worth it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: For our EPQ I only needed to produce a written report, but about halfway through I realised the most interesting findings were in the data visualisations, not the text. I taught myself to use basic chart tools and built an appendix of twelve illustrated graphs to go alongside the report. My supervisor said she'd never had a student do that before. I wasn't trying to impress her — I genuinely thought the graphs made the argument more honestly than words alone. It took an extra two weeks but I felt the work said what I actually wanted it to say.

commonMistakes: A story that's actually just meeting expectations, not exceeding them
Making the motivation sound like 'to get a better grade'
Going beyond in a way that's more about effort than genuine value added
followUps: Are there times when going beyond what's expected is the wrong call?

question: How do you cope when you're under a lot of academic pressure? Can you give us a real example?
lookingFor: Self-awareness about pressure management and a sustainable, mature approach — not just 'I work harder'.

bullets: Name a genuine high-pressure moment, not a generic situation
Describe the specific strategies you used — not just attitude
Reflect honestly on what worked and what you'd change
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: In the lead-up to my A-level mocks I hit a point where I was revising until midnight, sleeping badly, and retaining nothing. I realised I was performing busyness more than actually learning. I cut two hours off my revision each day, added a run in the afternoon, and started using active recall instead of re-reading. Within a week I was sleeping properly and my practice test scores improved. What I tell myself now is that output is a function of quality, not volume — the hardest thing was actually stopping when I felt like I should still be working.

commonMistakes: Describing an approach that sounds unsustainable at university level
Giving no real example — just stating a principle
Pretending pressure doesn't affect you
followUps: What's a sign, for you, that you're about to burn out — and what do you do when you notice it?

question: Tell us about a time you came up with a new idea that was actually used. How did it happen?
lookingFor: Creative thinking tied to real impact — not just having ideas, but translating one into something tangible.

bullets: Explain the problem or gap your idea addressed
Describe how you developed and pitched it
Show the impact and what you learned about bringing an idea to life
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I noticed our school's library stock didn't reflect the cultural backgrounds of a lot of students — the 'recommended reads' board hadn't changed in years. I proposed a student-curated shelf where anyone could nominate a book with a short card explaining why, and the library would purchase the top picks each term. I wrote a one-page proposal, the librarian loved it, and we launched it at the start of Michaelmas. By the end of the year the shelf had thirty books and a waiting list. The thing I found most interesting was that the idea wasn't radical — it just needed someone to put it in writing and ask.

commonMistakes: Describing an idea you suggested but was never implemented
Taking all the credit when the implementation relied on others
A story where the 'new idea' was fairly obvious and unoriginal
followUps: What stopped others from suggesting this idea before you?

question: Tell us about a time you were genuinely disappointed by a result. How did you pick yourself back up?
lookingFor: Emotional honesty plus forward motion — being disappointed is human; what matters is what you do next.

bullets: Name a real disappointment — not a thinly disguised success
Allow the emotional response before moving to recovery
Describe the concrete action you took and what it produced
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I was rejected from the national young science competition after months of preparation — my project didn't even make the regional shortlist. I was gutted, genuinely. I gave myself a week where I didn't look at the project at all. Then I read the judges' published comments, which were blunt about what they were looking for, and I realised my write-up had buried the novel finding. I entered a different competition three months later with a reworked version that led with the surprising result, and it placed in the top ten. The rejection was probably the best piece of feedback I've ever received, I just couldn't see it straight away.

commonMistakes: Rushing past the disappointment to show you 'handled it fine'
A story where the initial failure was actually quite minor
Recovery that was just time passing, with no active effort
followUps: Is there a failure you still haven't fully made peace with?

question: Tell us about a time you organised something from scratch. What did you do to make sure it worked?
lookingFor: Methodical, forward-thinking planning — anticipating problems, not just reacting to them.

bullets: Name what you were organising and the scale or complexity involved
Describe your planning process — how you broke it down and delegated
Reflect on what worked, what didn't, and what you'd do differently
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I organised a mock interview day for Year 12 students where local professionals came in for practice sessions. That meant sixty slots across twelve interviewers, transport, dietary requirements, briefing packs, and student preparation. I built a shared spreadsheet, assigned each task an owner and a deadline two days before it was actually needed, and ran a short rehearsal the day before. On the day one interviewer cancelled last minute, but because I'd thought through backup options in advance I swapped two slots without anyone noticing. The thing I was most pleased with was that I'd planned for things to go wrong, not just for things to go right.

commonMistakes: Describing a project that was really quite small and required little organisation
Claiming all credit when others did significant work
Saying everything went perfectly — that's rarely credible and shows no learning
followUps: What's the thing you most often forget to plan for?

question: Tell us about a time you respectfully challenged a decision made by someone in authority. What happened?
lookingFor: Confidence to speak up when it matters, paired with the maturity to do it constructively rather than confrontationally.

bullets: Name the decision and why you thought it was worth challenging
Describe how you raised it — the tone, the evidence, the channel
Be honest about the outcome, including if they didn't change course
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My PE teacher announced our team selection based on a single trial match, and I felt that had disadvantaged players who'd had a difficult week. I asked to speak to him privately rather than arguing in front of the group. I said I understood the decision was his to make, but asked whether there was room for a second trial given the circumstances. He explained his reasoning — he'd actually watched more than just that session — and I realised I'd had incomplete information. He didn't change the selection, but he appreciated that I'd come privately and constructively rather than undermining him. I learned that challenging a decision is sometimes about getting understanding rather than getting a different outcome.

commonMistakes: A story where you're clearly right and the authority figure is clearly wrong — that's too easy
Describing a confrontational approach that's framed as 'respectful'
Not acknowledging whether the challenge was actually justified
followUps: How do you decide when a decision is worth challenging and when it's better to let it go?

subtopic: Learning from a mentor or role model
question: Tell us about someone who has had a significant influence on how you think or approach problems. What did you take from them?
lookingFor: Reflective awareness of how you've been shaped — not just who inspired you, but what specifically changed in how you think or act.

bullets: Name a real person with a specific connection to your experience, not a famous figure
Describe one or two concrete things they taught you or showed you
Connect it to how you actually behave or think differently now
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My economics teacher had a habit of asking 'what would have to be true for that to be wrong?' every time a student stated something confidently. At first it was annoying. After a while it changed how I think — I now instinctively look for the counter-case before I commit to an argument. When I was writing my EPQ I used that question on my own hypothesis and found a gap I'd been glossing over, which made the final argument much stronger. The influence wasn't motivational, it was methodological, and I think that kind of influence lasts longer.

commonMistakes: Naming a historical figure or celebrity you've never actually interacted with
Describing admiration without any change in your own behaviour
Being so generic it could be anyone's answer
followUps: Have you ever passed that influence on to someone else?

question: Tell us about a time your group couldn't agree on something important. What did you do to help move things forward?
lookingFor: Constructive conflict resolution — the ability to create productive movement in a stuck group without steamrolling or withdrawing.

bullets: Name the specific disagreement and why it mattered for the group
Describe what you did to break the deadlock — structure, facilitation, compromise
Reflect on what made the resolution work or not work
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: For a group psychology project we couldn't agree on which case study to focus on — two people felt strongly about different topics and the rest of us were in the middle. I suggested we each take ten minutes to write down the three strongest arguments for our preferred option, share them without debate, and then vote. It sounds simple, but it took the argument out of the decision. We went with one option, and the two people who'd lost the vote were actually fine because they'd felt heard. I've used versions of that process since — the key was making the method feel fair before we applied it.

commonMistakes: Describing yourself as the decisive leader who overruled others
Saying you let someone else resolve it while you stayed neutral
No specific technique — just saying 'we talked it through'
followUps: What would you have done if the structured process hadn't worked either?

question: Tell us about an achievement you're genuinely proud of. Why does it matter to you?
lookingFor: An authentic account of success that reveals your values — the 'why it matters' tells the panel more than the achievement itself.

bullets: Name a specific achievement, not a vague category
Explain the effort or obstacle it required — earn the pride
Connect it to something you value, not just to how others perceived it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'm most proud of getting our school's first-ever open-mic night off the ground. Not because it was a big event — the turnout was small — but because six months earlier the idea had been dismissed twice and I'd been told students wouldn't engage. I kept a record of interest from peers, found a sponsor for the equipment, and ran it anyway. Watching three students perform original music in front of an audience for the first time made it worth every conversation I'd had to push through. I value it because it taught me that 'that won't work' is sometimes just 'no one's tried it yet.'

commonMistakes: An achievement the panel clearly doesn't believe matters to you — it sounds like bragging
A group achievement presented as purely personal
The achievement being entirely about external recognition rather than internal meaning
followUps: What would you do differently to make it even better?

subtopic: Balancing independence and asking for help
question: Tell us about a time you had to decide whether to work through a problem yourself or ask for help. How did you make that call?
lookingFor: Mature judgement about the limits of self-reliance — knowing when to persist alone and when asking for help is the smarter move.

bullets: Describe the specific problem and your initial instinct
Walk through how you decided which route to take
Reflect on whether you got the balance right
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Working on my maths coursework I hit a modelling problem I couldn't get past after two hours. My instinct was to keep going — I hate asking for help on something that feels like it should be within reach. But I checked myself: I had a deadline and I was going in circles. I sent a short, specific email to my teacher explaining exactly where I was stuck, and he replied in twenty minutes with a hint that unlocked it in another twenty. What I took from it is that there's a difference between giving up and knowing when your time is better spent learning from someone else's pointer. I could have spent another four hours and maybe got there — or I could make progress and understand it better for having it explained.

commonMistakes: Making it sound like you always ask for help immediately — that shows no self-reliance
Making it sound like you always persist alone — that shows no self-awareness
A situation where the call was actually obvious rather than genuinely balanced
followUps: How do you think university will challenge your current balance on this?

question: Tell us about a time you worked with someone whose background or perspective was very different from yours. What did you learn from it?
lookingFor: Genuine curiosity and openness to difference — evidence you can collaborate across backgrounds and come away richer for it.

bullets: Name the specific difference in background or perspective — be concrete, not vague
Describe how it shaped the work or the interaction
Show an honest, specific thing you learned that changed how you think
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: On a community history project I was paired with a student whose family had settled in our town as refugees — a background entirely unlike mine. We were mapping the same local area, but the places she chose to highlight and the stories she attached to them were completely different from mine, even for the same streets. Her perspective made me realise how much of what I'd called 'local history' was actually just the dominant community's history. The project was better for the clash of lenses. I'd gone in thinking we were documenting the same thing — I came out understanding there are always multiple histories being lived in the same place.

commonMistakes: Framing the other person's background as the interesting thing rather than what you actually learned
A story with no real tension or difference — it's too smooth to be meaningful
Generic 'I learned a lot' without naming a specific shift in thinking
followUps: How has that experience changed the way you approach new groups or collaborations?
